one_line_takeaway: ToolTweak shows that simply rewriting an MCP/agent tool's name and description via an iterative black-box LLM loop can lift its selection rate from ~20% to as high as ~82%, transferably across models, exposing a fairness-and-security vulnerability in agent tool marketplaces that paraphrasing only partially mitigates and perplexity filtering does not.
key_finding: ToolTweak raises average tool selection from a ~20% (uniform) baseline to as high as 81.6% (DeepSeek), with large gains on GPT-OSS (73.6%) while more robust models like Llama 3.1 rise only to ~34%. The attack transfers across models (victim architecture is the dominant factor; DeepSeek stays ~60% selectable regardless of which LLM crafted the attack) and exhibits a self-bias effect (a model's own generations attack it best). Most gains arrive within the first two iterations. Even a no-knowledge attacker reaches ~60% TSR. Paraphrasing meaningfully reduces bias (debiasing 60%-88% of tool distributions depending on model) but ToolTweak is notably more robust to it than the manual-suffix attack; perplexity filtering alone fails because attack and benign perplexity distributions overlap (though attacked descriptions are much longer, so joint length+perplexity features could feed a lightweight classifier). A striking secondary finding: a numeric suffix in the tool name alone biases selection (Qwen2.5-7B picks the '1'-suffixed tool 72.6% of the time, Llama3.2-3B 65.6%), suggesting ordinality in names implicitly signals ranking.
core_contribution: The paper introduces ToolTweak, a lightweight, gradient-free, black-box attack that iteratively rewrites a tool's name and description to bias an LLM agent's tool-selection process toward that tool over equally capable competitors. Framed as a discrete optimization over the tool's name/description tokens to maximize the probability the agent emits a call to the target tool across a query set, ToolTweak uses a PAIR-inspired loop (Algorithm 1): an attacker LLM proposes new metadata, the metadata is evaluated on a victim agent, and the resulting empirical selection rate (not a separate judge model) is fed back as the optimization signal for the next round (default K=10 iterations). The paper's own additional contributions are: (1) a comprehensive empirical study across six local and proprietary models; (2) analysis of the distributional impact on tool-usage (measured via Jensen-Shannon Divergence toward the attacker's one-hot ideal); and (3) two evaluated defenses -- paraphrasing (mitigation) and perplexity filtering (prevention). NOTE: the record's supplied 'abstract' text about MCP servers, rug-pull, and reading secrets/private messages is NOT this paper's contribution; it is a Related-Work citation to Beurer-Kellner & Fischer's tool-poisoning work. ToolTweak itself does not exfiltrate data -- it biases which tool is selected.
threat_model: The attacker is a legitimate tool provider competing with other tools in an online tool marketplace/registry (e.g., MCP or a commercial platform like Composio). Goal: make an LLM agent preferentially select the attacker's tool over functionally equivalent competitors. Capabilities: (1) Control -- can freely rewrite its own tool's name and description but cannot modify the fixed parameter schema; (2) Knowledge -- full visibility into the tool database but not necessarily the victim agent's underlying LLM/architecture (black-box); (3) Data access -- receives platform usage statistics (how often its tool is called vs competitors) plus a sample of user queries. The attack must persistently bias selection across many queries and contexts, which the authors argue is harder than a one-shot jailbreak. A restrictive 'no knowledge' variant (only usage-statistic feedback, no query or competitor-tool visibility) is also studied.
limitations: Authors' stated/evident limitations: (1) No retrieval layer -- experiments use small fixed tool sets (5 tools) presented directly in context, not large dynamic databases with embedding-based retrieval, which they flag as future work; (2) only the first tool call is counted, ignoring parallel/multi-tool calls; (3) black-box, gradient-free only -- white-box gradient-based attacks are left to future work; (4) perplexity defense is ineffective standalone and the stronger classifier idea reduces to AI-text detection, which is itself evadable; (5) even the knowledge-of-defense attacker does not fully recover base-attack performance, but the underlying vulnerability remains unsolved. Additional reviewer-noted caveats: results depend on temperature-0 victims that the authors themselves note are not truly deterministic across API providers; the manual-suffix baseline sometimes outperforms ToolTweak (e.g., Grok 89.5% vs 50.7%), so the attack's headline value is robustness-to-defense rather than raw strength; and attacker LLMs occasionally 'reward-hacked' the metric by copying competitor names.

datasets_used: ToolBench (real RapidAPI functions) -- 10 tasks x 5 candidate APIs, 100 generated queries per task, standardized to OpenAI JSON-schema
Berkeley Function Calling Leaderboard (BFCL v3 simple) -- used for the tool-name-bias and suffix/defense reproduction experiments
quantitative_results: Best Selection Rate under ToolTweak (Table 1): DeepSeek 81.6%, GPT-OSS 73.6%, Grok 3 Mini 50.7%, Gemini 48.7%, Qwen 2.5 45.9%, Llama 3.1 34.0% (vs ~18.9-20.2% No Attack)
Manual-suffix BSR (Table 1): Grok 89.5%, GPT-OSS 76.4%, DeepSeek 68.7%, Qwen 61.3%, Gemini 56.1%, Llama 38.1%

Paraphrase-defended TSR (Table 2): DeepSeek 48.6% (from 81.6% undefended), GPT-OSS 63.3% (from 73.6%), Grok3 Mini 32.7% (from 50.7%), Gemini 30.1% (from 48.6%), Qwen 34.3% (from 45.9%), Llama 27.2% (from 33.9%)
Attacking-the-defended-agent TSR (Table 2): DeepSeek 59.7%, GPT-OSS 67.9%, Grok 44.8%, Gemini 37.5%, Qwen 46.7%, Llama 34.4%
Paraphrase debiasing coverage: 88% of tool distributions for DeepSeek down to 60% for Qwen; only 42% for Llama 3.1
No-knowledge attack reaches ~60% TSR vs ~80% with full knowledge (DeepSeek)
Query-count ablation: TSR ~74.4% with 2 example queries rising to ~81.6% with 10; tool-count ablation ~73% with 1 tool to 81.6% with full set
Tool-name ordinality bias: '1'-suffixed tool selected 72.6% (Qwen2.5-7B) and 65.6% (Llama3.2-3B), averaged over presentation order, 5 trials
BFCL reproduction: augmented descriptions called <6.5x more than originals undefended; ~1.18x less than originals after paraphrase defense
baselines_compared: Original (unmodified) tool descriptions -- nominally 20% uniform selection across 5 tools, measured ~18.9-20.2% No Attack across models
Manual persuasive-suffix attack (simplified LLM-free version following Faghih et al. 2025)
